text: The surgeon identifies the anatomical plane near the bladder and begins dissection, applying steady traction to carefully separate the bladder from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon holds the bladder and continues dissection on its under surface, progressing slowly and steadily.
The surgeon lifts the peritoneum and makes an incision, revealing the bladder wall beneath. The bladder wall is visible as the peritoneum is cut.
The surgeon retroverts the bladder, adjusting its position to reveal a rounded structure on one side.
The surgeon visually identifies the bladder and fibers in front of the surgical field, noting the extent from the lateral bend. The cervix is visible, and the surgeon proceeds to cut the fibers.
The surgeon continues dissection by adhering to basic principles, moving from normal tissue areas to abnormal adhesions through a lateral window.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the bladder, with the bladder wall clearly visible after lifting and cutting the peritoneum.
